questions.question: Bone marrow consists of three primary components. Which of these is described as fat for a 'purpose unknown at this time'?
questions.detail: Yellow marrow is fundamentally fatty tissue, and its physiological purpose remains 'unknown at this time'.

questions.question: At what age is the conversion of red marrow to yellow marrow usually completed in the human body?
questions.detail: Marrow conversion is a developmental process that is typically complete by the mid-20s.

questions.question: In the normal pattern of marrow conversion, in which direction does the process move?
questions.detail: Normal conversion of red to yellow marrow occurs in a distal-to-proximal direction, starting in the extremities.

questions.question: Within a single long bone, which anatomical area converts from red to yellow marrow LAST?
questions.detail: The conversion sequence within a long bone is Epiphysis -> Diaphysis -> Distal Metaphysis -> Proximal Metaphysis.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered a normal variant of red marrow persistence in an adult?
choices: A. Red marrow in the mid-shaft of the tibia
B. Red marrow in the humeral and femoral heads
C. Red marrow in the distal phalanges
D. Red marrow in the calcaneal diaphysis
E. Red marrow in the radius
questions.detail: Small areas of red marrow in the humeral and femoral heads are recognized as normal variants in adults.

questions.question: What is considered the 'money sequence' for evaluating bone marrow on MRI?
questions.detail: T1-weighted MRI is the primary sequence for assessing marrow signal characteristics.

questions.question: On a T1-weighted MRI, how should normal red marrow signal compare to other internal structures?
choices: A. It should be brighter than yellow marrow
B. It should never be darker than a normal intervertebral disc or muscle
C. It should be as dark as cortical bone
D. It should be brighter than subcutaneous fat
E. It should be darker than joint fluid
questions.detail: Normal red marrow signal intensity on T1 should always be equal to or greater than that of muscle or a normal intervertebral disc.

questions.question: Marrow 'reconversion' (yellow back to red) occurs in the exact reverse order of normal conversion. Where does this process begin?
choices: C. Axial skeleton (spine and flat bones)
questions.detail: Marrow reconversion follows the reverse path of conversion, initiating in the axial skeleton.

questions.question: Patchy areas of red marrow in the proximal femoral metaphysis is a normal variant seen in which population?
questions.detail: In teenagers, patchy red marrow signal in the proximal femoral metaphysis is a classic normal finding.

questions.question: 'Distal femoral sparing' of red marrow is a normal variant classically seen in teenagers and:
questions.detail: Menstruating women often demonstrate red marrow sparing in the distal femur, which is considered normal.

questions.question: On a sagittal T1 MRI of the spine, you identify marrow that is darker than the adjacent (non-desiccated) disc. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: If marrow is darker than the adjacent disc on T1, it is a pathologic sign of an infiltrative process like leukemia.

questions.question: Multiple Myeloma is a notable exception to the rule that infiltrative conditions affect marrow diffusely. How does it typically present on MRI?
choices: D. Marrow is brighter than fat
questions.detail: Unlike many infiltrative processes, Multiple Myeloma often appears as multifocal, speckled deposits on MRI.

questions.question: A 'Chloroma' (Granulocytic Sarcoma) is best described as a destructive mass in the bone of a patient with:
questions.detail: A chloroma is a solid, destructive accumulation of leukemic cells occurring in bone or soft tissue.

questions.question: In ultrasound, 'Anisotropy' occurs when the sound beam is:
choices: B. NOT perpendicular to the tendon
C. Parallel to the sound beam
questions.detail: Anisotropy is an ultrasound artifact that occurs when the beam is not perpendicular to the orientation of the tendon fibers.

questions.question: Anisotropy is most problematic and common when imaging which tendon as it curves along the humeral head?
choices: E. Long head of the biceps
questions.detail: The curvature of the supraspinatus tendon makes it highly susceptible to anisotropy artifacts during shoulder ultrasound.

questions.question: Why is anisotropy considered a 'pain in the ass' for radiologists?
choices: A. It makes the tendon look brighter (hyperechoic) than normal
B. It can make a normal tendon look hypoechoic, mimicking injury
C. It only occurs in obese patients
D. It requires expensive software to correct
E. It prevents the visualization of bone
questions.detail: Anisotropy causes normal tendons to appear hypoechoic (darker), which can be mistaken for a tear or tendinosis.

questions.question: On ultrasound, a normal tendon appears:
questions.detail: A normal, healthy tendon is characteristically hyperechoic (bright) on ultrasound.

questions.question: Tendon tears on ultrasound are typically identified as:
choices: C. Areas of increased thickness with no signal change
E. Areas of posterior acoustic enhancement
questions.detail: Focal areas of decreased echogenicity (hypoechoic) are the standard ultrasound markers for tendon tears.

questions.question: To diagnose 'Plantar Fasciitis' on ultrasound, the thickness of the plantar fascia should be greater than:
questions.detail: Thickness exceeding 4 mm is the recognized ultrasound diagnostic threshold for plantar fasciitis.

questions.question: Which band of the plantar fascia is most commonly involved in Plantar Fasciitis?
choices: D. All bands are involved equally
questions.detail: Plantar fasciitis is most frequently localized to the central band of the fascia.

questions.question: 'Calcific Tendonitis' on ultrasound classically demonstrates which of the following?
choices: B. Shadowing, similar to a gallstone
E. Increased Doppler flow in the calcium
questions.detail: Calcium deposits within a tendon cause significant acoustic shadowing on ultrasound, similar to gallstones.

questions.question: Where is the most common site for Calcific Tendonitis (HADD) to be identified by ultrasound?
choices: E. Biceps tendon in the groove
questions.detail: The supraspinatus tendon is the most frequent anatomical site for hydroxyapatite deposition disease (HADD).

questions.question: When performing a joint procedure, what is the 'actual' target for the needle?
choices: C. The bone within the capsule
E. The skin directly over the joint
questions.detail: To ensure intra-articular needle placement, the operator must touch bone that is located within the joint capsule.

questions.question: During an arthrogram, why is it critical to avoid putting air into the joint?
choices: A. It causes immediate joint infection
B. It leads to patient discomfort
C. It causes susceptibility artifact on MRI
D. It prevents contrast from flowing
E. It makes the joint appear too small
questions.detail: Intra-articular air causes significant magnetic susceptibility artifacts ('blooming') on subsequent MRI images.

questions.question: For a hip arthrogram, what is the specific target for needle advancement?
choices: A. The center of the femoral head
B. The superior femoral head-neck junction
questions.detail: The superior aspect of the femoral head-neck junction is the standard target for hip joint access.

questions.question: In which position is the hip capsule the widest, making it the ideal target for a joint injection?
questions.detail: The joint capsule is anatomically widest at the femoral head-neck junction.

questions.question: What is the standard 'cocktail' volume typically injected for a hip arthrogram?
questions.detail: A total volume of approximately 14 cc is commonly used for hip arthrography cocktails.

questions.question: What is the recommended amount of Gadolinium (Gd) used in a standard hip or shoulder arthrogram cocktail?
questions.detail: Standard protocols utilize 0.1 cc of Gadolinium in a dilute cocktail for arthrography.

questions.question: To localize the target for a shoulder arthrogram, how should the patient's hand be positioned?
questions.detail: Placing the hand in supination (external rotation) helps open the joint space for the procedure.

questions.question: For a shoulder arthrogram, the needle should be directed toward the junction of which portion of the humeral head?
choices: D. Directly into the bicipital groove
questions.detail: The standard needle target for a shoulder arthrogram is the junction of the middle and inferior thirds of the humeral head.

questions.question: When performing a shoulder arthrogram, after striking bone, what is the next technical step?
choices: A. Inject the full volume immediately
B. Pull back 1 mm and turn the bevel toward the humeral head
C. Advance another 5 mm into the bone
questions.detail: After touching bone, pulling back slightly and orienting the bevel allows contrast to flow into the capsule.

questions.question: How can a radiologist confirm the needle is in the joint capsule before injecting the full arthrogram cocktail?
choices: A. The patient reports severe pain
B. A small amount of contrast is injected and flows away from the tip
E. Blood is seen in the syringe
questions.detail: Confirmation of intra-articular placement is established when contrast flows freely away from the needle tip.

questions.question: What is the total approximate volume of the cocktail injected for a shoulder arthrogram?
questions.detail: A total volume of approximately 12 cc is injected for a standard shoulder arthrogram.

questions.question: If a patient is allergic to Gadolinium but needs a labral evaluation, what is the best alternative procedure?
questions.detail: A CT arthrogram using iodinated contrast is the preferred alternative for evaluating the labrum in patients with Gd allergies.

questions.question: If a patient is allergic to CT contrast but needs a steroid/lidocaine injection for pain management, how can the radiologist confirm needle placement?
choices: B. Inject air to confirm placement
questions.detail: If CT contrast is contraindicated, a small amount of air can be used to confirm intra-articular placement for a pain injection.

questions.question: Why is injecting air contraindicated for an arthrogram in a patient allergic to CT contrast?
choices: A. It causes a 'big blooming mess' on MRI
B. It causes an air embolus
C. It prevents the steroid from working
E. It mimics a labral tear
questions.detail: Air must not be used if the patient is proceeding to MRI, as it creates large blooming artifacts that obscure the anatomy.

questions.question: Marrow signal that turns yellow is often a response to:
choices: B. Stress or degenerative change in the spine
questions.detail: Adult fatty (yellow) marrow conversion is often triggered by chronic stress or degenerative changes, particularly in the spine.

questions.question: In an infant younger than 18 months, why can osteomyelitis spread from the metaphysis to the epiphysis?
choices: A. The growth plate is not yet formed
B. Transphyseal vessels are still present
C. Infants have higher marrow pressure
D. The epiphysis has no blood supply
E. It actually cannot spread in infants
questions.detail: Persistent transphyseal vessels in infants allow infection to bypass the growth plate and reach the epiphysis.

questions.question: After what age do the transphyseal vessels usually atrophy, leading to the 'septic tank' effect in the metaphysis?
questions.detail: By 18 months of age, these vessels typically atrophy, confining early hematogenous infection to the metaphysis.

questions.question: On MRI, what is considered the definitive sign of 'healed' osteomyelitis?
choices: C. Return of normal fatty marrow signal on T1
E. Enhancement of the previous abscess cavity
questions.detail: The restoration of normal bright fatty signal on T1-weighted MRI is the definitive imaging hallmark of a healed infection.

questions.question: Which 'buzzword' look is associated with the T2/STIR MRI appearance of ACL Mucoid Degeneration?
questions.detail: 'Celery stalk' describes the striated, thickened appearance of the ACL in mucoid degeneration on T2 images.

questions.question: The 'T1 buzzword' for ACL Mucoid Degeneration is:
questions.detail: On T1 images, the ACL in mucoid degeneration is often described as having a 'drumstick' appearance.

questions.question: A 'Stenosing Tenosynovitis' around the ankle, specifically involving the FHL tendon, is common in patients with:
questions.detail: Os Trigonum Syndrome is classically associated with inflammation and stenosis around the FHL (Harry) tendon.

questions.question: What ultrasound finding is diagnostic for 'Trigger Finger'?
choices: A. Fluid in the extensor sheath
E. Anisotropy of the A2 pulley
questions.detail: Ultrasound reveals a thickened, scarred flexor tendon sheath at the A1 pulley in trigger finger.

questions.question: In the 'Pretzel Principle' of forearm fractures, if an isolated fracture of the proximal ulna is seen, what must the radiologist do next?
choices: C. Order a CT of the humerus
questions.detail: According to the forearm ring principle, a proximal ulna fracture (Monteggia) necessitates an elbow X-ray to check for radial head dislocation.

questions.question: 'Rice bodies' are infarcted synovium seen in TB infection of joints and:
questions.detail: Rice bodies are characteristic features of both chronic tuberculous arthritis and end-stage Rheumatoid Arthritis.

questions.question: Which 'Aunt Minnie' clinical history is associated with an avulsion of the calcaneal tuberosity?
questions.detail: Calcaneal tuberosity avulsion fractures are an 'Aunt Minnie' radiographic sign for underlying Diabetes.

questions.question: In the 'Classic Flat Foot Progression,' what is the first structure to fail?
questions.detail: Failure of the Posterior Tibial Tendon (PTT) is the primary initiator of the flat foot collapse sequence.

questions.question: An MRI showing a 'Boomerang-shaped' Peroneus Brevis tendon is diagnostic of:
questions.detail: A boomerang-shaped or 'C-shaped' Peroneus Brevis tendon on axial MRI indicates a longitudinal split tear.

questions.question: 'Pelvic Congestion Syndrome' is most commonly tested using which clinical demographic?
choices: A. Elderly men with prostate issues
D. Infants with congenital heart disease
E. Post-menopausal women on estrogen therapy
questions.detail: The classic board-tested demographic for pelvic congestion syndrome is depressed, multiparous, pre-menopausal women.
